`dataset/Bank/telemetry/2021_03_05/metric/`).\n\n- The telemetry data in those subdirectories is stored in CSV format (e.g., `dataset/Bank/telemetry/2021_03_05/metric/metric_container.csv`).\n\n## DATA SCHEMA\n\n1.  **Metric Files**:\n    \n    1. `metric_app.csv`:\n\n        ```csv\n        timestamp,rr,sr,cnt,mrt,tc\n        1614787440,100.0,100.0,22,53.27,ServiceTest1\n        ```\n\n    2. `trace_span.csv`:\n\n        ```csv\n        timestamp,cmdb_id,parent_id,span_id,trace_id,duration\n 3160896K(4089472K), 0.1985754 secs] [Times: user=0.59 sys=0.00, real=0.20 secs] "\n        ```\n\n## POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSE REASONS:\n        \n- high CPU usage\n- high memory usage \n- network latency \n- network packet loss\n- high disk I/O read usage \n- high disk space usage\n- high JVM CPU load \n- JVM Out of Memory (OOM) Heap\n\n## POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSE COMPONENTS:\n\n- apache01\n- apache02\n- Tomcat01\n- Tomcat02\n- Tomcat04\n- Tomcat03\n- MG01\n- MG02\n- IG01\n- IG02\n- Mysql01\n- Mysql02\n- Redis01\n- Redis02\n\n## CLARIFICATION OF TELEMETRY DATA:\n\n1. This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8).\n- method is TranAD\n- output_folder_name is determined by current date, e.g., if today is 2025-11-17, then the output_folder_name is 1204\n- output_suffix is determined by start_ts and end_ts, e.g., start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), end_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8), then output_suffix is 1900_1930.\n- Do NOT invent values.\n\nTools auto-compute baselines, filter noise, and align to the given window.\n\n---\n\n## **Strict Prohibitions**\n\n- ❌ Never skip any Stage 1 tool.  \n- ❌ Never assume component names, KPIs, or topology—let tools discover them.  \n- ❌ Never select a healthy or downstream-only component as root cause.  \n- ❌ Never generate or suggest code.  \n- ❌ Never ignore INFO logs or treat logs/traces as secondary.\n\n--- \n\n\nThe issue you are going to solve is:\n\nDuring the specified time range of March 7, 2021, from 11:00 to 11:30, a failure was detected in the system. 1...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]

  "Cluster 1": "Cluster #1 occurs at 11:00–11:01 and contains 40 anomalies. Concurrently, multiple backend components show metric anomalies: Mysql02 exhibits high CPU usage across multiple cores, low free buffer pool pages, and elevated open files, suggesting resource exhaustion. Redis01 shows sudden spikes in network traffic, memory usage, and CPU utilization at 11:01, while Tomcat02 records increased disk read activity. Trace data reveals a broad spike in call frequency from UNKNOWN_PARENT to nearly all major services (IGs, Tomcats, dockers) at 11:00:58, implying a flood of incoming requests. This cluster likely represents a flash-crowd event or misrouted traffic surge overwhelming the system at ingress, with IG01 as the first failure point under load.",
  "Cluster 2": "Cluster #2 spans 11:03–11:13 (658 seconds) with 57 anomalies and centers on performance degradation due to sustained GC pressure and timeouts. IG01 logs show repeated GC-related events (PatternIDs like _103, _111, _17) starting at 11:05, indicating frequent garbage collection. IG02 and MG01 report rising JVM heap memory usage at 11:04, preceding increased disk I/O on sdb for IG01 and MG01 by 11:08–11:11. Mysql02 experiences a burst of write operations and lock contention at 11:10, reflected in metrics like 'Innodb data written' and 'MaxTrxRowsModified'. Apache02 shows high disk busy percentage at 11:10. The trace anomaly at 11:13:58 (MG02→dockerB1) suggests downstream RPC issues. This cluster reflects a cascading slowdown initiated by memory pressure in IG02 and MG01, leading to thread stalls, database contention, and eventual timeout propagation through the call chain.",
  "Cluster 3": "Cluster #3 (11:17–11:23) includes 27 anomalies focused on intermittent GC events and disk I/O surges. IG01 logs show recurring error/failure patterns (e.g., PatternID_121, _21) at 11:18 and _34 at 11:22. IG01 and IG02 exhibit periodic spikes in sdb write throughput around 11:18–11:23, indicating background flushing or logging bursts. Mysql01 shows late-stage fsync and write anomalies at 11:23. Redis01 reports widespread filesystem capacity and availability warnings at 11:17 across multiple mount points, suggesting a sudden metadata scan or monitoring sweep. Tomcat04 logs high CPU load at 11:18. No significant upstream trace anomalies are seen. This cluster appears to be a secondary ripple effect—possibly triggered by delayed cleanup tasks after Cluster #2—with Redis01’s filesystem alerts pointing to a potential configuration or monitoring script issue.",
  "Cluster 4": "Cluster #4 (11:25–11:30) is the most severe, with 449 anomalies and keywords OOM, Timeout, GC. At 11:25, IG01 begins logging OOM-related GC events (PatternID_0: CMS Initial Mark), followed by a massive wave of similar GC and failure logs peaking at 11:28. By 11:28, Mysql01 shows extreme container-level stress: nearly all Docker containers under it report abnormal CPU, memory, and network usage. JVM heap usage and thread counts spike across IG01, Mysql01, and others. ServiceTest1–11 all show degraded response rates (sr, rr) and increased latency (mrt) at 11:29–11:30. Trace data shows synchronized frequency drops across *all* service-to-service calls (IG→Tomcat, Tomcat→MG, MG→docker, etc.) at 11:29:58, indicating systemic unresponsiveness. This cluster represents a full-blown out-of-memory collapse originating in IG01's JVM, triggering cascading GC storms, process freezing, and communication breakdown across the entire system. The root cause is IG01’s memory exhaustion, which propagates downstream due to blocked threads and connection pooling failure."
}

This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type OSLinux-OSLinux_LOCALDISK_LOCALDISK-sda_DSKWrit...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]
2025-12-10 12:13:34.818 | INFO     | rca.baseline.rag_tool_multicandidate_agent.executor:execute_act:215 - execute LLM Final Answer:
{
  "Cluster 1": "Cluster #1 (13:00–13:01) shows a brief spike of 10 anomalies centered on the IG01 gateway and database components. Logs from IG01 indicate 'Error/Failure' patterns at exactly 13:00:00, coinciding with MySQL performance issues (Opened Tables, Table open cache misses). Simultaneous disk write activity on Mysql02 and network bandwidth utilization on Redis01 suggest downstream propagation. The trace anomalies from dockerA1/dockeraA2 to MG01 also occur at 13:00:58, indicating immediate downstream impact. This points to an initial failure at IG01 triggering cascading effects across the data tier.",
  "Cluster 2": "Cluster #2 (13:09–13:12) contains 14 anomalies with keywords 'GC, Error/Failure'. IG01 logs show GC-related errors (PatternID_20, 70, 132, 138) starting at 13:09:00. These coincide with rising CPU load on MG02 and increased TCP CLOSE-WAIT connections on both MG01 and MG02, suggesting connection leaks or thread exhaustion during garbage collection. Redis01 exhibits CPU, memory, and network packet anomalies at 13:11:00. Since GC logs precede metric spikes by up to 2 minutes and affect upstream gateways first, the root cause is likely excessive GC pressure originating in IG01, impacting downstream MG and Redis instances.",
  "Cluster 3": "Cluster #3 (13:16–13:20) involves 18 anomalies, again linked to 'GC, Error/Failure'. IG01 logs show multiple GC events (e.g., PatternID_16, 122, 116) beginning at 13:16:00. This coincides with memory pressure on MG02 (HeapMemoryUsed, HeapMemoryUsage) and elevated CPU on Redis01. A single trace anomaly from dockerB1→MG02 at 13:15:58 slightly precedes the cluster, but the sustained log and memory anomalies on IG01 and MG02 are more significant. The pattern suggests recurring GC cycles in the gateway (IG01) propagating memory and processing load downstream, with MG02 struggling under heap pressure.",
  "Cluster 4": "Cluster #4 (13:22–13:30) is the most severe, with 370 anomalies and keywords 'GC, OOM, Error/Failure'. Starting at 13:22:00, IG01 logs show repeated GC and allocation failure messages (PatternID_9, 125, etc.), continuing through to OOM indicators at 13:30:00. IG01’s CPU and thread usage spike steadily from 13:24 onward. At 13:24:00, Redis01 records a massive wave of anomalies—spanning JVM heap, container resources, MySQL metrics, filesystem, network, and disk—all timestamped simultaneously. This indicates a system-wide resource collapse triggered by uncontrolled memory growth. The earliest sustained anomalies are in IG01 logs and metrics, making it the root cause. The synchronized explosion of metrics across Redis01 suggests it became overwhelmed by requests or data from the failing IG01, culminating in an out-of-memory cascade."
}

This points to an initial failure at IG01 triggering cascading effects across the data tier....

Tomcat01\n- Tomcat02\n- Tomcat04\n- Tomcat03\n- MG01\n- MG02\n- IG01\n- IG02\n- Mysql01\n- Mysql02\n- Redis01\n- Redis02\n\n## CLARIFICATION OF TELEMETRY DATA:\n\n1. This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type Attribute: PatternID_135\n      ...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]
2025-12-10 12:49:07.632 | INFO     | rca.baseline.rag_tool_multicandidate_agent.executor:execute_act:215 - execute LLM Final Answer:
{
  "Cluster 1": "Cluster #1 (17:30–17:46 CST) is characterized by a surge in GC and memory-related anomalies, primarily centered on IG01. Multiple log patterns (PatternIDs 12, 19, 27, 33) indicate frequent 'GC (Allocation Failure)' and 'Full GC' events starting at 17:44 CST, immediately preceded by sustained high CPU and memory usage on IG01 and MG01. Metric anomalies show IG01 experiencing elevated disk read and total memory consumption at 17:36 CST, while MG01 exhibits rising heap memory usage from 17:31 CST onward. The earliest sustained anomaly is on MG01’s JVM heap usage, but the logs on IG01—occurring just before broader system-wide metric anomalies on downstream components like Mysql02 and Redis02—are more temporally aligned with the cascade trigger. IG01 is upstream in the call chain and shows correlated anomalies across logs, metrics, and trace frequency drops. Therefore, IG01 is identified as the root cause component due to its position, early timing of severe GC logs, and upstream dependency role.",
  "Cluster 2": "Cluster #2 (17:48–18:00 CST) is dominated by timeout-related issues following the earlier memory stress. The key symptom is a spike in request timeouts detected in ServiceTest7 and ServiceTest9 at 17:55 CST. These are correlated with renewed load on IG01's JVM memory at 17:51 CST and increased CPU and disk I/O on Mysql01 and Mysql02 around 17:56–17:57 CST. Trace data shows reduced call frequencies to Tomcat01, dockerA1, and others at 17:46:58 CST, suggesting degraded service propagation. While database-level disk write and DSKWTps anomalies appear during this window, they occur after the initial application-layer timeout signals. The earliest sustained anomaly is IG01’s JVM memory usage increase at 17:51 CST, which precedes the app-level timeouts. Given that IG01 is an upstream gateway and its memory pressure resurfaces just before the timeout wave, it remains the primary contributing component, indicating failure to recover from Cluster #1, leading to cascading timeouts downstream."
}

This intensive GC activity caused significant CPU usage in the JVM, degrading application performance.\nMitigation Principles: Optimize JVM heap settings (e.g., adjust NewRatio, MaxTenuringThreshold) to reduce frequency of Full GC events.; Enable and analyze GC logs regularly to detect early signs of memory pressure.; Consider migrating to a more efficient garbage collector like G1GC for large heaps.; Implement proactive alerting on repeated Full GC occurrences to enable faster response before CPU saturation.", 'metadata': {'source_file': 'bank_root_cause_reports_en.jsonl', 'original_source': 'IG01 failure at 2021-03-04 21:06:00', 'system_type': 'Banking Microservice (pod)', 'component': 'IG01', 'metrics': ['CPU', 'GC', 'load', 'memory', 'rr', 'trace', 'utilization'], 'root_cause_category': 'The root cause of the high JVM CPU load on pod IG01 is a Full Garbage Collection (GC) event triggered by memory allocation failure, which led to a CMS'}, 'score': 0.3712995648384094}]

2025-12-10 12:49:13.018 | INFO     | rca.baseline.rag_tool_multicandidate_agent.controller:control_loop:260 - controller RAG Cluster 2 result: [{'content': 'Component: IG01\nEvent: IG01 failure at 2021-03-04 22:25:00\nSystem Type: Banking Microservice (pod)\nSymptoms: JVM Out of Memory (OOM) Heap; Log pattern ID 100 shows a ThreadLocal leak detected at 22:25:00 where a web application failed to remove a ThreadLocal with a LinkedList value, indicating a potential memory leak.; Log pattern IDs 87 and 88 show warnings about active request processing threads failing to finish during shutdown, increasing likelihood of memory leaks.; Log pattern ID 106 and ID 112 indicate illegal access attempts to load classes after the web application was stopped, confirming that threads were still running post-shutdown.\nAffected Metrics: CPU, GC, heap, load, memory, rt, thread\nRoot Cause Category: The root cause of the failure is a JVM Out of Memory (OOM) heap condition in pod IG01, triggered by a memory leak due to improper cleanup of ThreadLoc\nFailure Pattern: The root cause of the failure is a JVM Out of Memory (OOM) heap condition in pod IG01, triggered by a memory leak due to improper cleanup of ThreadLocal variables and long-running requests during application shutdown. This led to continuous memory consumption, eventual garbage collection thrashing, and process termination.\nMitigation Principles: Review and fix code in the UOCP web application to ensure ThreadLocal variables are properly removed in finally blocks or via cleanup hooks.; Investigate and optimize long-running database queries or transactions that prevent timely shutdown of request-processing threads.; Increase heap memory limits temporarily while fixing the root code issue, and tune JVM garbage collection settings for better OOM resilience.; Implement proactive monitoring for ThreadLocal leaks and pre-OOM symptoms such as sustained high JVM CPU and frequent GC events.; Enforce graceful shutdown timeouts and ensure all worker threads terminate within the grace period to prevent memory leaks during redeployment.', 'metadata': {'source_file': 'bank_root_cause_reports_en.jsonl', 'original_source': 'IG01 failure at 2021-03-04 22:25:00', 'system_type': 'Banking Microservice (pod)', 'component': 'IG01', 'metrics': ['CPU', 'GC', 'heap', 'load', 'memory', 'rt', 'thread'], 'root_cause_category': 'The root cause of the failure is a JVM Out of Memory (OOM) heap condition in pod IG01, triggered by a memory leak due to improper cleanup of ThreadLoc'}, 'score': 0.43411120772361755}]

In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as Attrib...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]

{
  "Cluster 1": "Cluster 1 contains 10 anomalies occurring between 09:30 and 09:32 CST, primarily involving disk I/O metrics on IG02 (sdb read/transfer rates) and JVM uptime. These are isolated events with no sustained pattern or corroborating log anomalies. The MySQL instance Mysql01 also shows a brief anomaly in 'Innodb pages created'. Given the short duration, lack of recurrence, and absence of upstream/downstream propagation, these are likely transient resource spikes rather than root causes.",
  "Cluster 2": "Cluster 2 spans from 09:34 to 10:00 CST with 1175 anomalies, dominated by GC-related logs (PatternID_12, _13, _25) on IG01 starting at 09:37–09:40, signaling memory pressure. This is followed by sustained JVM heap usage anomalies in IG01, IG02, Redis01, and Redis02 beginning around 09:45, meeting the sustained anomaly threshold. Trace data shows increased latency and frequency in calls to Tomcat02 and MG01 during this period. Per RCA rules, IG01 exhibits the earliest sustained metric anomalies post-GC logs, acts as an upstream caller, and correlates with downstream degradation. Therefore, IG01 is identified as the root cause component due to garbage collection-induced memory stress propagating through the call chain."
}

\n- network packet loss\n- high disk I/O read usage \n- high disk space usage\n- high JVM CPU load \n- JVM Out of Memory (OOM) Heap\n\n## POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSE COMPONENTS:\n\n- apache01\n- apache02\n- Tomcat01\n- Tomcat02\n- Tomcat04\n- Tomcat03\n- MG01\n- MG02\n- IG01\n- IG02\n- Mysql01\n- Mysql02\n- Redis01\n- Redis02\n\n## CLARIFICATION OF TELEMETRY DATA:\n\n1. This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type \'all\' needs to be used in Stage 1.\n- date_offline is 2021_03_05 for Bank dataset\n- date_online should be determined by issue description date\n- start_ts and end_ts should be determined by issue description time scope, e.g., if the description is "the specified time range of March 8, 2021, from 19:00 to 19:30", then start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), start_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8).\n- method is TranAD\n- output_folder_name is determined by current date, e.g., if today is 2025-11-17, then the output_folder_name is 1204\n- output_suffix is determined by start_ts and end_ts, e.g., start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), end_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8), then output_suffix is 1900_1930.\n- Do NOT invent values.\n\nTools auto-compute baselines, filter noise, and align to the given window.\n\n---\n\n## **Strict Prohibitions**\n\n- ❌ Never skip any Stage 1 tool.  \n- ❌ Never assume component names, KPIs, or topology—let PatternID_109\n       Timestamps: 1615271700 (2021-03-09 14:35:00 CST), 1615272180 (2021-03-09 14:43:00 CST)\n     • Entity: IG01 | Attribute: PatternID_114\n       Timestamps: 1615272420 (2021-03-09 14:47:00 CST)\n     • Entity: I...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]
