\n- network packet loss\n- high disk I/O read usage \n- high disk space usage\n- high JVM CPU load \n- JVM Out of Memory (OOM) Heap\n\n## POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSE COMPONENTS:\n\n- apache01\n- apache02\n- Tomcat01\n- Tomcat02\n- Tomcat04\n- Tomcat03\n- MG01\n- MG02\n- IG01\n- IG02\n- Mysql01\n- Mysql02\n- Redis01\n- Redis02\n\n## CLARIFICATION OF TELEMETRY DATA:\n\n1. This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type \'all\' needs to be used in Stage 1.\n- date_offline is 2021_03_05 for Bank dataset\n- date_online should be determined by issue description date\n- start_ts and end_ts should be determined by issue description time scope, e.g., if the description is "the specified time range of March 8, 2021, from 19:00 to 19:30", then start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), start_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8).\n- method is TranAD\n- output_folder_name is determined by current date, e.g., if today is 2025-11-17, then the output_folder_name is 1204\n- output_suffix is determined by start_ts and end_ts, e.g., start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), end_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8), then output_suffix is 1900_1930.\n- Do NOT invent values.\n\nTools auto-compute baselines, filter noise, and align to the given window.\n\n---\n\n## **Strict Prohibitions**\n\n- ❌ Never skip any Stage 1 tool.  \n- ❌ Never assume component names, KPIs, or topology—let tools discover them.  \n- ❌ Never select a healthy or downstream-only component as root cause.  \n- ❌ Never generate or suggest code.  \n- ❌ Never ignore INFO logs or treat logs/traces as secondary.\n\n--- \n\n\nThe issue you are going to solve is:\n\nDuring the specified time range of March 9, 2021, from 18:00 to 18:30, a failure was detected within the system. 161528...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]

{
  "Cluster 1": "Cluster #1 (18:00–18:02) shows a brief spike of 51 anomalies across multiple systems, primarily at the start of the observation window. Key entities include IG01, Mysql01, and Tomcat-related components. Most anomalies are isolated timestamp events (e.g., CPU, disk, uptime) with no sustained pattern. Trace frequency spikes occur from UNKNOWN_PARENT to IG01, IG02, and Tomcat instances at 18:02:31, suggesting a sudden increase in request volume. However, due to lack of sustained anomalies or preceding log evidence, this cluster is interpreted as an upstream traffic surge—likely originating from apache load balancers—causing momentary system-wide ripple effects. No internal root cause is evident; likely triggered by external client activity.",
  "Cluster 2": "Cluster #2 (18:04–18:23) contains 477 anomalies, heavily concentrated logs and metrics centered on IG01 and Mysql01 at 18:13. IG01 exhibits repeated GC and OOM-related log patterns (PatternIDs 5, 10, 13, etc.) starting at 18:06, peaking at 18:13 and 18:18. These coincide with memory metric anomalies: JVM heap usage and used memory spike on IG01 at 18:13 and 18:14. Concurrently, downstream Mysql01 shows widespread anomalies across filesystem, Docker containers, database connections, thread counts, and network at 18:13. Trace data shows increased duration and frequency from UNKNOWN_PARENT→IG01 and dockerA1→MG01 at 18:13:31, indicating propagation of latency. Per RCA rules: IG01 has sustained, early-starting anomalies with log triggers preceding metric degradation. Thus, IG01 is the root cause—suffering JVM memory exhaustion leading to GC storms—which cascaded downstream to MG, Docker, and MySQL layers.",
  "Cluster 3": "Cluster #3 (18:25–18:27) includes 24 anomalies, mainly metric-based. Redis02 shows sudden disk, filesystem, and thread count anomalies at 18:25, along with trace frequency increases from UNKNOWN_PARENT to IG02 and Tomcat01. IG01 logs show PatternID_30 and _108 at 18:27, possibly response timeouts. However, these logs follow rather than precede the Redis/Mysql anomalies. IG02 shows DSKTps spike at 18:25—same time as Redis02’s disk reads. Given Redis02's sustained disk I/O and thread anomalies, and its role in Tomcat and MG caching, this cluster points to Redis02 as the root cause: a storage performance degradation triggering cascading delays in dependent services.",
  "Cluster 4": "Cluster #4 (18:29–18:30) has only 4 transient anomalies: IG02 disk activity, Mysql01 TCP-FIN-WAIT rise, and CPU utilization shifts on apache01. All occur within a 60-second window with no sustained pattern or corroboration. These are interpreted as recovery or cleanup artifacts following earlier clusters—specifically post-Redis02 resolution—and thus considered symptoms rather than root causes."
}

This was triggered by abnormal disk re\nFailure Pattern: The root cause of the incident is high disk I/O read usage on pod MG01, which began at 2021-03-06 04:57:00 CST. This was triggered by abnormal disk read operations on both sda and sdb block devices, particularly reflected in increased DSKReadWrite, DSKTps, and DSKRead metrics. The issue likely originated from downstream database activity, as Mysql01 exhibited anomalies in connection count, temporary table creation, and handler writes at the exact failure timestamp (04:56:00), suggesting a sudden surge in database load that propagated to MG01's storage layer.\nMitigation Principles: Investigate query patterns on Mysql01 around 04:56:00 to identify inefficient queries causing excessive disk reads on dependent services.; Implement disk I/O monitoring and alerting at the pod level, especially for critical components like MG01.; Optimize database configuration to reduce temporary table creation and improve caching.; Consider adding read replicas or scaling storage performance for MG01 if disk load is recurring.; Conduct regular load testing to simulate and prepare for traffic spikes originating from upstream services.", 'metadata': {'source_file': 'bank_root_cause_reports_en.jsonl', 'original_source': 'MG01 failure at 2021-03-06 04:56:00', 'system_type': 'Banking Microservice (pod)', 'component': 'MG01', 'metrics': ['CPU', 'I/O', 'connection', 'disk', 'latency', 'load', 'rt', 'trace'], 'root_cause_category': 'The root cause of the incident is high disk I/O read usage on pod MG01, which began at 2021-03-06 04:57:00 CST. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as Red...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]

  "Cluster 3": "From 19:36 to 19:48 CST, sustained anomalies emerged starting at 19:36 with MG01's JVM heap usage increase. Concurrently, Tomcat metrics (heap usage, request errors, session issues) degraded. Log anomalies on IG01 (PatternID_135, _136) appeared at 19:38, just before Redis/MG degradation. Given that IG logs precede sustained JVM and database load, and IG is upstream, the root cause likely stems from abnormal client requests processed by IG, propagated to Tomcat and MG, ultimately overloading Redis and MySQL resources.",
  "Cluster 4": "At 19:53 CST, IG01 logged PatternID_82 and _131 (indicating service errors), immediately followed by filesystem fullness alerts on Redis01 (root and /home partitions). Apache01 also showed CPU idle drops at 19:52. The timing suggests that prior system overload (from Cluster 3) led to disk space exhaustion, triggering new failures. The root cause is likely the ongoing resource exhaustion originating earlier, now manifesting as storage saturation and service errors.",
  "Cluster 5": "At 19:56 CST, IG01 reported three log patterns (_62, _67, _80)—including thread leaks and misconfigured connection pools—shortly after MG02’s JVM heap usage spiked. These logs suggest application-level instability in IG’s startup/shutdown processes. However, these occur after sustained anomalies in Clusters 3 and 4. Thus, they are symptoms of instability rather than root causes, possibly triggered by recovery attempts or service restarts during ongoing resource stress.",
  "Cluster 6": "From 19:58–20:00 CST, IG01 again logged errors (PatternID_90, _124), while multiple systems (Redis01, Mysql01, apache01/02) reported Zabbix agent anomalies (process count, uptime). These appear to be monitoring health checks failing due to system overload or restarts. Apache CPU idle dropped at 19:59, suggesting residual load. This cluster reflects system recovery attempts or instability persisting after the main incident, with no new root cause identified."
}

Concurrently, Tomcat metrics (heap usage, request errors, session issues) degraded. Log anomalies on IG01 (PatternID_135, _136) appeared at 19:38, just before Redis/MG degradation. Given that IG logs precede sustained JVM and database load, and IG is upstream, the root cause likely stems from abnormal client requests processed by IG, propagated to Tomcat and MG, ultimately overloading Redis and MySQL resources....

Tomcat01\n- Tomcat02\n- Tomcat04\n- Tomcat03\n- MG01\n- MG02\n- IG01\n- IG02\n- Mysql01\n- Mysql02\n- Redis01\n- Redis02\n\n## CLARIFICATION OF TELEMETRY DATA:\n\n1. This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8).\n- method is TranAD\n- output_folder_name is determined by current date, e.g., if today is 2025-11-17, then the output_folder_name is 1204\n- output_suffix is determined by start_ts and end_ts, e.g., start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), end_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8), then output_suffix is 1900_1930.\n- Do NOT invent values.\n\nTools auto-compute baselines, filter noise, and align to the given window.\n\n---\n\n## **Strict Prohibitions**\n\n- ❌ Never skip any Stage 1 tool.  \n- ❌ Never assume component names, KPIs, or topology—let Timestamp...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]

you:\n\n## TELEMETRY DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:\n\n- You can access the telemetry directory in our microservices system: `dataset/Bank/telemetry/`.\n\n- This directory contains subdirectories organized by a date (e.g., `dataset/Bank/telemetry/2021_03_05/`). \n\n- Within each date-specific directory, you’ll find these subdirectories: `metric`, `trace`, and `log` (e.g., `dataset/Bank/telemetry/2021_03_05/metric/`).\n\n- The telemetry data in those subdirectories is stored in CSV format (e.g., `dataset/Bank/telemetry/2021_03_05/metric/metric_container.csv`).\n\n## DATA SCHEMA\n\n1.  **Metric Files**:\n    \n    1. `metric_app.csv`:\n\n        ```csv\n        timestamp,rr,sr,cnt,mrt,tc\n        1614787440,100.0,100.0,22,53.27,ServiceTest1\n        ```\n\n    2. `metric_container.csv`:\n\n        ```csv\n        timestamp,cmdb_id,kpi_name,value\n        1614787200,Tomcat04,OSLinux-CPU_CPU_CPUCpuUtil,26.2957\n        ```\n\n2.  **Trace Files**:\n\n    1. `trace_span.csv`:\n\n        ```csv\n        timestamp,cmdb_id,parent_id,span_id,trace_id,duration\n        1614787199628,dockerA2,369-bcou-dle-way1-c514cf30-43410@0824-2f0e47a816-17492,21030300016145905763,gw0120210304000517192504,19\n        ```\n\n3.  **Log Files**:\n\n    1. `log_service.csv`:\n\n 3160896K(4089472K), 0.1985754 secs] [Times: user=0.59 sys=0.00, real=0.20 secs] "\n        ```\n\n## POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSE REASONS:\n        \n- high CPU usage\n- high memory usage \n- network latency \n- network packet loss\n- high disk I/O read usage \n- high disk space usage\n- high JVM CPU load \n- JVM Out of Memory (OOM) Heap\n\n## POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSE COMPONENTS:\n\n- apache01\n- apache02\n- Tomcat01\n- Tomcat02\n- Tomcat04\n- Tomcat03\n- MG01\n- MG02\n- IG01\n- IG02\n- Mysql01\n- Mysql02\n- Redis01\n- Redis02\n\n## CLARIFICATION OF TELEMETRY DATA:\n\n1. This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as given]\n\nNote that:\n- the value of pipeline_type can be \'all\' or a single pipeline (\'Bank_metric_container\', \'Bank_metric_app\', \'Bank_trace\', and \'Bank_log\'), and generally pipeline_type \'all\' needs to be used in Stage 1.\n- date_offline is 2021_03_05 for Bank dataset\n- date_online should be determined by issue description date\n- start_ts and end_ts should be determined by issue description time scope, e.g., if the description is "the specified time range of March 8, 2021, from 19:00 to 19:30", then start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), start_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8).\n- method is TranAD\n- output_folder_name is determined by current date, e.g., if today is 2025-11-17, then the output_folder_name is 1204\n- output_suffix is determined by start_ts and end_ts, e.g., start_ts is 1615201200 (2021/03/08 19:00:00 UTC+8), end_ts is 1615203000 (2021/03/08 19:30:00 UTC+8), then output_suffix is 1900_1930.\n- Do NOT invent values.\n\nTools auto-compute baselines, filter noise, and align to the given window.\n\n---\n\n## **Strict Prohibitions**\n\n- ❌ Never skip any Stage 1 tool.  \n- ❌ Never assume component names, KPIs, or topology—let tools discover them.  \n- ❌ Never select a healthy or downstream-only component as root cause.  \n- ❌ Never generate or suggest code.  \n- ❌ Never ignore INFO logs or treat logs/traces as secondary.\n\n--- \n\n\nThe issue you are going to solve is:\n\nDuring the time range of March 10, 2021, from 00:00 to 00:30, there was one failure detected in the system. Example: \'Execute anomaly detection on service user-service for metrics CPU and latency between 14:30 and 15:00.\' Do NOT include multi-step plans."\n}\n\nLet\'s begin.'}, {'role': 'user', 'content': "Note: when you use tools, please strictly follow the format: {'pipeline_type': 'all', 'date_offline': '2021_03_05', 'date_online': '2021_03_10', 'start_ts': 1615305600, 'end_ts': 1615307400, 'method': 'TranAD', 'output_folder_name': '1204', 'output_suffix': '0000_0030'}. Please identify the exact root cause occurrence datetime.\n\nPlease first confirm the issue description time scope. Entity: IG01 | Attribute: PatternID_104\n       Timestamps: 1615306320 (2021-03-10 00:12:00 CST)\n     • Entity: IG01 | Attribute: PatternID_109\n     ...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]
2025-12-10 16:42:02.580 | INFO     | rca.baseline.rag_tool_multicandidate_agent.executor:execute_act:215 - execute LLM Final Answer:
{
  "Cluster 1": "The root cause of the anomaly cluster is a garbage collection (GC) surge on IG01, starting at 2021-03-10 00:17:00 CST. This is evidenced by a sustained spike in GC-related log patterns (e.g., 'Allocation Failure', 'CMS Initial Mark') and correlated CPU user time anomalies on IG01 beginning at 00:17:00 and peaking at 00:19:00–00:24:00. The GC pressure triggered cascading resource stress across downstream systems: Tomcat instances (Tomcat02, Tomcat04) showed memory free drops from 00:12:00 onward; MG01 and MG02 exhibited JVM heap usage anomalies at 00:19:00 and 00:20:00; and Dockerized services (dockerA1) reported memory limit issues by 00:24:00. Disk I/O and filesystem anomalies on apache01, Tomcat, and Mysql nodes followed after 00:20:00, indicating propagation of load into storage and database layers. Despite isolated issues on other nodes, IG01 is the earliest sustained anomaly source with upstream position in the call chain, confirming it as the root cause."
}

This led to application pauses, degraded performance, and cascading delays in request processing. The memory pressure originated from uncollected objects due to improper cleanup of ThreadLocals and long-running database connection threads during application shutdown, culminating in an OutOfMemoryError and subsequent JVM-level GC thrashing.\nMitigation Principles: Optimize JVM heap settings and garbage collector configuration for IG01 to handle peak load and reduce GC pause times.; Fix memory leaks by ensuring all background threads and ThreadLocal variables are properly cleaned up during application shutdown.; Implement proactive monitoring of GC frequency and heap utilization to trigger alerts before OutOfMemoryError occurs.; Conduct code review on UOCP application shutdown hooks to ensure graceful termination of resources.; Introduce synthetic transaction monitoring to detect end-to-end latency degradation linked to backend GC events.", 'metadata': {'source_file': 'bank_root_cause_reports_en.jsonl', 'original_source': 'Tomcat02 failure at 2021-03-04 19:22:00', 'system_type': 'Banking Microservice (pod)', 'component': 'Tomcat02', 'metrics': ['GC', 'connection', 'heap', 'latency', 'memory', 'network', 'rr', 'rt', 'thread', 'trace'], 'root_cause_category': 'The root cause of the network latency issue in Tomcat02 is a full garbage collection (GC) event triggered by a Java heap memory exhaustion in the IG01'}, 'score': 0.40409791469573975}]

This microservice system is a banking platform.\n\n2. The `metric_app.csv` file only contains four KPIs: rr, sr, cnt, and mrt,. In contrast, `metric_container.csv` records a variety of KPIs, such as CPU usage and memory usage. The specific names of these KPIs can be found in the `kpi_name` field.\n\n3. In different telemetry files, the timestamp units and cmdb_id formats may vary:\n\n- Metric: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787440).\n\n- Trace: Timestamp units are in milliseconds (e.g., 1614787199628).\n\n- Log: Timestamp units are in seconds (e.g., 1614787201).\n\n4. Please use the UTC+8 time zone in all analysis steps since system is deployed in China/Hong Kong/Singapore.\n\n# **Failure Diagnosis Rules (Tool-Based Mode)**\n\nYou are a DevOps assistant orchestrating pre-built tools to diagnose failures in a microservice system. **Do not generate code**—only decide which tools to invoke and in what order.\n\n## **1. Anomaly Detection (Multi-Telemetry Evidence Gathering)**  \nRun **all the following** telemetry analysis tools **in sequence**, using identical time-window parameters:\n\n- **1.1 Metric Analysis**  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_app` for application-level symptoms (e.g., latency, success rate drops).  \n  - Use `Bank_metric_container` for infrastructure/resource symptoms (e.g., CPU, memory pressure).\n\n- **1.2 Trace Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_trace`, regardless of metric results, to detect:  \n    - Error spans, latency outliers, broken call chains, or abnormal retry patterns.\n\n- **1.3 Log Analysis**  \n  - Always run `Bank_log` to identify:  \n    - Bursts of ERROR/WARN logs, recurring exceptions (e.g., “timeout”), or operational anomalies (e.g., failover messages).\n\n- **1.4 Anomaly Consolidation**  \n  - Run `Bank_cluster_window` to cluster anomalies across components and signals within the 30-minute window, revealing fault propagation timelines.\n\n> ✅ All Stage 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4) tools must be executed, none are optional.  \n> ✅ Output: Structured anomaly timelines per telemetry type.\n\n---\n\n## **2. Fault Identification**\n\n- **2.1 Generate Candidate Faults**  \n  - Extract candidate faults from `Bank_cluster_window` output. Each includes:  \n    - Component ID, affected signals (metrics/traces/logs), and anomalous time interval(s).\n\n- **2.2 Apply RAG per Candidate**  \n  - For each candidate, use its attributes to query the RAG system for historical incident matches and causal hypotheses.\n\n- **2.3 Select Top Faults**  \n  - Rank candidates by RAG similarity score.  \n  - Keep top 1-10 candidates (with RAG results) for root cause analysis.\n\n---\n\n## **3. Root Cause Localization**\n\nInput: Top 1-10 candidate faults from Stage 2.\n\nApply **four criteria** to identify true root cause(s):\n\n- **3.1 Temporal Primacy**  \n  - Keep only candidates whose anomaly onset is among the **earliest** (±30 sec tolerance).  \n  - *Root causes precede their effects.*\n\n- **3.2 Topological Causality**  \n  - Use call graph from `Bank_trace` to prune downstream symptoms:  \n    - If an upstream component is also anomalous and earlier, the current candidate is **not** the root.  \n  - Allow multiple root causes only if they are **topologically independent**.\n\n- **3.3 RAG Semantic Confidence**  \n  - Prefer candidates with:  \n    - High RAG similarity (>0.8),  \n    - Clear causal narratives (e.g., “DB connection pool exhausted”),  \n    - Historical confirmation as root cause.\n\n- **3.4 Multi-Telemetry Activeness**  \n  - Require **convergent active-failure evidence** from ≥2 telemetry types:  \n    - **Metrics**: Resource saturation or internal error spikes (not just latency).  \n    - **Traces**: Self-originated errors (not inherited).  \n    - **Logs**: Causal messages (e.g., “Connection refused”), not generic timeouts.\n    \nApply **two key** Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Decision Rules to finalize root cause(s):\n- **Primary Root Cause Prioritization Rule**\n    - When identifying the root cause of a system anomaly, the event that satisfies both of the following conditions shall be designated as the root cause:\n      - It has the earliest occurrence timestamp among all candidate events.\n      - It is associated with the largest quantity of anomaly indicators (e.g., performance metrics, error signals, system alerts).\n- **Log Data Weight Adjustment Rule**\n    - In the root cause assessment process, the weighting coefficient of log-derived clues shall be reduced by default. The only exception is when the corresponding log entry has an occurrence time that precedes all other anomaly indicators (in which case its weight shall be restored to the standard level for root cause validation).\n\n### **3.5 Output**\n- Final root cause component(s).  \n- Supporting evidence:  \n  - Earliest anomaly window,  \n  - Topological role (root vs. symptom),  \n  - Key RAG-matched incident & hypothesis,  \n  - Convergent telemetry signals.\n\n---\n\n## **Tool Invocation Requirements**\n\nAll tools require:\n- `date_offline`: Baseline date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `date_online`: Failure date (YYYY_MM_DD)  \n- `start_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `end_ts`: Unix timestamps (UTC+8)  \n- `method`: TranAD  \n- `output_folder_name`: [as given]  \n- `output_suffix`: [as 04:40:00 CST)\n     • Entity: IG01 ...\n\n============================================================\n.\n# How to Generate General Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Process for Microservices with Multimodal Data\nFor microservice fault RCA using logs, metrics, and traces, follow this streamlined 4-step framework, leveraging the system’s dependency chain to pinpoint root causes:\n\n# Call Chain Description (Technical Narrative)\n\nThe observed trace reveals the following end-to-end service invocation pattern:\n\n1. An external request enters the system through the **IG** (Integration Gateway).  \n2. The IG forwards the request to a **Tomcat** application server for business logic processing.  \n3. During execution, Tomcat invokes a downstream **MG** (Microservice Gateway) to access core microservices.  \n4. The MG, in turn, interacts with services running inside **Docker** containers—either by calling containerized workloads or delegating tasks to them.  \n5. Additionally, there is a reverse interaction where **Docker-hosted components initiate calls back to the MG**, suggesting a bidirectional integration pattern (e.g., event-driven callbacks or sidecar coordination).\n\n## Complete Core Dependency Chain (Including Invisible/Embedded Components)\n**Client → apache (Load Balancing) → IG → Tomcat (⇆ Redis) → MG (⇆ Redis) ⇄ Dockerized Services (→ Mysql)**\n\n### Component-by-Component Explanation and Breakdown\n1. **Client → apache**\napache (apache01/apache02) serves as the **invisible entry point** at the foremost tier of the entire call chain. Although it is a mandatory pass-through for all external client requests, it is not captured in distributed trace data. This is because the trace collection probes are not deployed on the apache load balancers, which only undertake basic request forwarding and traffic distribution without handling business logic, thus failing to generate traceable spans.\n\n2. **apache → IG**\nThe IG layer (IG01/IG02, interface gateway services) acts as the **first traceable root node** in the distributed tracing system. It receives requests forwarded by the apache load balancers and executes core gateway functions such as request authentication, protocol conversion, and service routing before relaying the requests to the downstream Tomcat business layer. All requests entering the backend microservice cluster are first recorded as trace spans at the IG tier.\n\n3. **IG → Tomcat**\nThe Tomcat tier (Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04) is the **core business logic layer** of the banking system, responsible for processing key business workflows like transaction verification and user information management. The notation `(⇆ Redis)` indicates that Tomcat services have bidirectional embedded dependencies on Redis (Redis01/Redis02). Specifically, Tomcat instances query Redis for cached data (e.g., user sessions, transaction configurations) during business processing to improve response efficiency. However, this Redis access is treated as an **internal resource call** rather than an inter-service RPC invocation, so it does not generate independent trace spans and is only reflected in the latency metrics of Tomcat’s trace spans.\n\n4. **Tomcat → MG**\nThe MG tier (MG01/MG02, microservice governance middleware) provides service governance capabilities such as service registration and discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed transaction coordination for the system. Similar to the Tomcat tier, the `(⇆ Redis)` annotation here denotes that MG components invoke Redis to retrieve configuration information or distributed lock resources during request routing and permission verification. This Redis dependency is also an internal resource access and is not captured as a separate trace span in the distributed tracing system.\n\n5. **MG ⇄ Dockerized Services**\nThe bidirectional arrow (`⇄`) represents the **mutual interaction** between the MG middleware and containerized business services (e.g., dockerB1): the MG tier forwards standardized business requests to containerized services, and the containerized services return processing results to the MG tier. The suffix `(→ Mysql)` indicates that containerized services ultimately call the Mysql database (Mysql01/Mysql02) for persistent data storage (e.g., transaction ledger recording, account data writing). If trace probes are not deployed on the Mysql client of containerized services, this database access will not generate independent trace spans and will only be counted as part of the latency of containerized service spans.\n\n- IG might be IG01 or IG02\n- MG might be MG01 or MG02\n- apache might be apache01 or apache02\n- Tomcat might be Tomcat01, Tomcat02, Tomcat03, or Tomcat04\n- docker might be dockerA1, dockerA2, dockerB1, or dockerB2\n- Mysql might be Mysql01 or Mysql02\n\nNotably, the mutual dependency between **MG** and **Docker** implies that the MG not only orchestrates calls to containerized services but may also expose endpoints consumed by those same containers—common in modern cloud-native designs involving control planes, agent-based monitoring, or asynchronous task processing.\n\nApply these RCA Decision Rules:\n- **Sustained Anomaly Priority**: Prefer entities with ≥3 anomalies on the same metric within 5 minutes.\n- **Earliest Among Sustained**: If multiple sustained anomalies exist, pick the one starting earliest.\n- **Log as Trigger Only**: Treat log anomalies as high-confidence only if they precede sustained metric anomalies by ≤2 minutes.\n- **Upstream over Downstream**: Favor components that are called by others (per trace data) and fail first.\n- **Ignore Isolated Events**: Single-point anomalies without corroboration are likely symptoms.\n\nYou MUST respond ONLY with a JSON object containing the following keys:\n{\n  "Cluster 1": "...",\n  "Cluster 2": "...",\n  ...\n}\n        '}]

This appears to be a transient fluctuation without significant impact, possibly a temporary load spike or monitoring artifact, and not root cause-worthy per isolation rules.",
  "Cluster 3": "Cluster #3 (04:51–05:00 CST) reflects ongoing system instability following Cluster #1. Multiple error logs (e.g., PatternID_107, 125) occur at 04:53 on IG01, preceded by trace frequency anomalies at 04:51. Sustained CPU idleness anomalies appear on Mysql02 and MG01 starting at 04:52, with IG01 showing CPU issues at 04:55. The pattern suggests residual stress from the earlier incident, with IG01 continuing to surface errors and downstream components struggling with resource utilization. However, as these anomalies follow Cluster #1 and lack new upstream triggers, they are likely symptoms rather than an independent root cause."
